
# Exercise 4.2.2

```{=typst}


#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.2.2.#n)], supplement: none)

Build the matrix $B$ for a three-node bar element with nodal coordinates $(x_1, y_1)$, $(x_2, y_2)$, and $(x_3, y_3)$.

== Solution

We can estimate the displacement with the use of shape functions:

$
  bold(u)(x,y,z) approx bold(N)(x,y,z)bold(U)^((e))
$

Where $bold(U)^((e))$ is a collection of the displacement degrees of freedom.

Analysing a single node in its x displacement, it is true that

$
  u_x(x,y,z) = sum_(i=1)^n N_(i(x,y,z)) u_(i x) 
$

Deriving in relation to x:

$
  epsilon_(x x) = (partial u_x) / (partial x) = sum_(i=1)^(n) (partial N_i) / (partial x) u_(i x)
$

Thus, the $bold(B)$ matrix represents an element to generalize the relation between the strain vector and displacement matrix:

$
  bmat(
    epsilon_(x x);
    epsilon_(y y);
    gamma_(x y);
  )
  =
  bold(B) dot
  bmat(
    u_(1x);
    u_(1y);
    u_(2x);
    u_(2y);
    dots.v;
    u_(n x);
    u_(n y)
  )
$ 

which is simply $bold(epsilon) = bold(B) bold(U)^((e))$. $bold(B)$ on itself can be represented as a union of matrixes:

$
  bold(B)
  =
  mat(bold(B)_1, bold(B)_2, bold(B)_3, bold(B)_4, bold(B)_5, dots.h, bold(B)_n)
$

Since our element can only suffer strain from compression/traction - that is, we assume it to be that of a truss, our equilibrium will be

 $ epsilon_(s s) = bold(B) bold(U)^((e)) = bmat(bold(B)_1, bold(B)_2, bold(B)_3) bmat(u_(1x); u_(1y); u_(2x); u_(2y); u_(3x); u_(3y)) $

Where

$
  bold(B)_i = mat((partial N_i) / (partial x), (partial N_i) / (partial y))
$

To define $bold(B)_i$, we can start from the strain definition

$
  epsilon_(s s) = (partial u_s) / (partial s)
$

We know that, via the chain rule

$
  (partial N_i) / (partial s) = (d N_i) / (d xi) dot (d xi) / (d s)
$ <partial>

$
  J = ||bold(J)|| = ||mat((d x) / (d xi), (d y) / (d xi))^T|| = sqrt(((d x) / (d xi))^2+((d y) / (d xi))^2) = (d s) / (d xi)
$
Hence
$
  1 / J = (d xi) / (d s)
$ <subto>

Substituting #ref(<subto>) into #ref(<partial>)  we get

$
  (partial N_i) / (partial s) = 1 / J (d N_i) / (d xi) 
$
$
  bold(B)_i = mat((partial N_i) / (partial x), (partial N_i) / (partial y))
  =
  mat(
    (partial N_i) / (partial s) (partial x) / (partial xi) (partial xi) / (partial s),
    (partial N_i) / (partial s) (partial y) / (partial xi) (partial xi) / (partial s),
  )
  =
  (partial N_i) / (partial s) (partial xi) / (partial s) mat((partial x) / (partial xi), (partial y) / (partial xi))

$

Which finally equates to

$
  bold(B)_i = 1 / (J^2) (d N_i) / (d xi) mat((partial x) / (partial xi), (partial y) / (partial xi))
$



```

# Exercise 4.2.3

```{=typst}



#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.2.3.#n)], supplement: none)

Find the matrix $bold(B)$ for a three-node triangular element with nodal coordinates $(x_1, y_1)$, $(x_2, y_2)$, and $(x_3, y_3)$. Why is the matrix $bold(B)$ constant along the element, and what does that imply?

== Solution

First of all, a 3 node triangular element is defined by the following shape functions

$
  bold(N) = mat(
    1 - xi - eta ;
    xi;
    eta;
  )
$

For 

$
  bold(x) = sum_(i=1)^(3) N_i (xi, eta) dot bold(x)_i
$

$
  bold(u) = sum_(i=1)^(3) N_i (xi, eta) dot bold(u)_i
$

$
  bmat(
    epsilon_(x x);
    epsilon_(y y);
    gamma_(x y);
  )
  =
  bold(B) dot
  bmat(
    u_(1x);
    u_(1y);
    u_(2x);
    u_(2y);
    u_(3 x);
    u_(3 y)
  )
$ 

$
  bold(B) = mat(bold(B)_1, bold(B)_2, bold(B)_3)
$

$
  bold(B)_i
  =
  mat(
    (partial N_i) / (partial x), 0;
    0, (partial N_i) / (partial y);
    (partial N_i) / (partial y), (partial N_i) / (partial x)
  )
$

It is known that

$
  (partial bold(N)_i) / (partial bold(x)) = (partial bold(N)_i) / (partial bold(xi)) bold(J)^(-1)
$

$
  x(xi, eta) = (1 - xi - eta) x_1 + xi x_2 + eta x_3 = x_1 + xi(x_2 - x_1) + eta(x_3 - x_1)
$

$
y(xi, eta) = (1 - xi - eta) y_1 + xi y_2 + eta y_3 = y_1 + xi(y_2 - y_1) + eta(y_3 - y_1)
  
$

Thus the Jacobian is

$
  bold(J)
  =
  mat(
    x_2 - x_1, y_2 - y_1;
    x_3-x_1, y_3-y_1
  )
  =
  mat(
    x_(21), y_(21);
    x_(31), y_(31);
  )
$

$
  J = det(bold(J)) = x_21 y_31 - x_31 y_21 = (x_2 - x_1) (y_3 - y_1) - (x_3 - x_1)(y_3 - y_1) 
$

Which, as seen by the shoelace formula, $J = 2A$.

It is known that
$
  bold(J)^(-1) = ("adj"(bold(J))) / (det(bold(J)))
  =
  1/ (2A)
  mat(
    y_31, -y_21;
    -x_31, x_21 
  )
$

For the first node

$
  mat(
    (partial N_1) / (partial x);
    (partial N_1) / (partial y);
  )
  =
  bold(J)^(-1)
    mat(
    (partial N_1) / (partial xi);
    (partial N_1) / (partial eta);
  )
  =
  1/ (2A)
  mat(
    y_31, -y_21;
    -x_31, x_21 
  )
  mat(-1;-1)
$

$
  mat(
    (partial N_1) / (partial x);
    (partial N_1) / (partial y);
  )
  =
  1 / (2A) mat(
    y_21 - y_31;
    x_31 - x_21
  )
  =
  1 / (2A)
  mat(
    y_23;
    x_32
  )
$

For the second node


$
  mat(
    (partial N_2) / (partial x);
    (partial N_2) / (partial y);
  )
  =
  bold(J)^(-1)
    mat(
    (partial N_2) / (partial xi);
    (partial N_2) / (partial eta);
  )
  =
  1/ (2A)
  mat(
    y_31, -y_21;
    -x_31, x_21 
  )
  mat(1;0)
$

$
  mat(
    (partial N_2) / (partial x);
    (partial N_2) / (partial y);
  )
  =
  1 / (2A) mat(
    y_31;
    x_13
  )
$

For the third node

$
  mat(
    (partial N_3) / (partial x);
    (partial N_3) / (partial y);
  )
  =
  1 / (2A) mat(
    y_12;
    x_21
  )
$

Thus, finally:

$
  bold(B) =
  1 / 2A
  dot
  mat(
    y_23, 0, y_31, 0, y_12, 0;
    0, x_32, 0, x_13, 0, x_21;
    x_32, y_23, x_13, y_31, x_21, y_12;
  )
$

Note the notation $x_(i j) = x_i - x_j$.

The strain-displacement matrix $bold(B)$ is uniform across the entire domain of the element $Omega_e$:

$ bold(B)(x,y) = "const" quad forall (x,y) in Omega_e $

This characteristic is true because the Jacobian $J$ is constant, because the element is linear.

```

# Exercise 4.2.4

```{=typst}


#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.2.4.#n)], supplement: none)

For the quadrilateral element shown below, and given its nodal coordinates and nodal-displacement vector

$ C = mat(0, 0; 4, 2; 4, 4; 0, 2) "m" quad "and" quad U^((e)) = mat(0; 0; 0.01; 0.01; 0.015; 0.015; 0; 0.015) "m" $

as shown in the figure. Approximate the strain vector at the natural coordinates $(xi, eta) = (-sqrt(1/3), sqrt(1/3))$.

== Solution

First step, is to define the shape functions and its Jacobian
$
  bold(N)(xi, eta)
  =
  mat(
    ((1 - xi)(1 - eta)) / (4);
    ((1 + xi)(1 - eta)) / (4);
    ((1 + xi)(1 + eta)) / (4);
    ((1 - xi)(1 + eta)) / (4);
  )
$

Or simply

$
  N_i (xi, eta)
  =
  1/4
  (xi_i - xi)(eta_i - eta)
$

Taking its derivatives

$
  mat(
    (partial N_i) / (partial xi);
    (partial N_i) / (partial eta);
  )
  =
  mat(
    eta - eta_i;
    xi - xi_i;
  )
$

#let C = (
  (0, 0),
  (4, 2),
  (4, 4),
  (0, 2),
)

$
  mat(
    x(xi, eta);
    y(xi, eta)
  )
  =
  sum_(i=1)^n
  mat(
     x_i / 4 (xi_i - xi) (eta_i - eta);
     y_i / 4 (xi_i - xi) (eta_i - eta)
  )
$

The strain vector for a two dimensional element, which can suffer strains in xx and yy, and the shear strain xy, is defined by


$
  bmat(
    epsilon_(x x);
    epsilon_(y y);
    gamma_(x y);
  )
  =
  mat(bold(B)_1, bold(B)_2, bold(B)_3, bold(B)_4)
  dot
  bmat(
    u_(1x);
    u_(1y);
    u_(2x);
    u_(2y);
    u_(3x);
    u_(3y);
    u_(4x);
    u_(4y);
  )
$

To better solve this problem, we can use Julia





```

# Exercise 4.2.5

```{=typst}

#set math.equation(numbering: n => [(4.2.5.#n)], supplement: none)

Find an expression for the matrix $B$ of a three-node bar element in 2D as a function of the Jacobian and the local coordinate $xi$. Consider $epsilon = (partial u) / (partial s)$, where $s$ is the curvilinear coordinate along the bar path.

== Solution
solution text

```
